text: The video shows a hiatal hernia at the top of the screen. A cautery instrument is used to dissect fatty tissue from the hernia sac, progressively removing more tissue to prepare for the repair.

The robotic system precisely dissects tissue around the diaphragm, esophagus, and stomach with minimal bleeding, providing a clear three-dimensional view. Tiny blood vessels are cauterized and sealed effectively. The dissection progresses around both sides of the esophagus, with care taken to avoid injuring the visible nerve at the top of the screen. An instrument is placed behind the esophagus to facilitate mobilization for the upcoming wrap placement.

The robotic view shows the diaphragm crura on the left and right, bordering the hiatal hernia. At the top of the screen, the pulsating white vessel is identified as the aorta. Small blood vessels on the stomach's exterior, known as short gastric vessels, are divided using a vessel sealer, which seals and divides tissue simultaneously, ensuring a bloodless field. The space behind the stomach is visible, and complete mobilization of the upper stomach is performed in preparation for the fundoplication.

The surgeon continues dissection, taking down blood vessels attached to the spleen and nearby fatty tissue using robotic instruments. The thin tissue layer is divided with minimal blood loss. The robotic instruments demonstrate wristing movements, allowing precise manipulation around the tissue, which is not possible with traditional laparoscopic techniques.

The surgeon passes both sides of the stomach behind the esophagus to create a wrap, preparing for suturing. The diaphragm crura are then closed with sutures, carefully re-approximating the muscle without crushing the tissue. The hernia defect is closed with a few sutures, eliminating the hernia without the need for mesh reinforcement.
